coredata.dc:title: Enhancing the macroscopic polarization of CdS for piezo-photocatalytic water splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Meiyu
Nie, Siyang
Cheng, Tao
Feng, Yu
Zhang, Chenchen
Zheng, Lei
Wu, Liang
Hao, Weichang
Ding, Yong
coredata.dc:description: Piezo-photocatalytic hydrogen production from water splitting is emerging as a promising way to generate renewable energy, but is challenged by low efficiency and ambiguous understanding of mechanisms. Herein, the CdS phase junction (H/C-CdS) with interfacial structural distortion is applied for prominent piezo-photocatalytic H2 evolution for the first time. Density functional theory (DFT) and second harmonic generation (SHG) demonstrate that the superior macroscopic polarity in H/C-CdS originates from the distortion of CdS4 tetrahedron units at the interface between hexagonal CdS (H-CdS) and cubic CdS (C-CdS). The obvious enhanced macroscopic polarity of H/C-CdS largely boosts the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes with the elimination of the electrostatic screening produced in CdS phase junction. An encouraging piezo-photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of 3.19 mmol·g−1·h−1 is delivered by H/C-CdS in pure water in the absence of any co-catalyst, which is 1.4 and 5.3 times higher than those of H-CdS (2.31 mmol·g−1·h−1) and C-CdS (0.59 mmol·g−1·h−1), respectively. Additionally, the value-added oxidation product H2O2 is produced in piezo-photocatalysis. This work firstly discloses macroscopic polarization enhancement induced by phase junction system on improvement of piezo-photocatalytic H2 evolution of CdS.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Macroscopic polarization
Dipole moment
CdS phase junction
Piezo-photocatalytic
H2 evolution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of the as prepared catalysts. (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of H/C-CdS. (b) XRD patterns of H-CdS, H/C-CdS and C-CdS. (c) UV–vis DRS spectra of H-CdS, H/C-CdS and C-CdS. (d) TG-DTA curves of H/C-CdS. (e) 3D morphology of H/C-CdS nanoparticle.

The identification of CdS phase junction. (a) HRTEM image of H-CdS. (b) The partial enlarged drawing of H-CdS from figure (a). (c) and (d) HRTEM images of H/C-CdS. (e) The partial enlarged drawing of H/C-CdS from figure (d). (f) HRTEM image of H/C-CdS.

The investigation of interfacial structure for H/C-CdS. High-resolution XPS spectra of (a) Cd 3d for H-CdS, H/C-CdS and C-CdS, (b) S 2p for H-CdS, H/C-CdS and C-CdS. (c) S L-edge X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) spectroscopy of H-CdS, H/C-CdS and C-CdS. (d) S K-edge X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) spectroscopy of H-CdS and H/C-CdS.

Piezo-photocatalytic H2 and H2O2 production. (a) The piezo-photocatalytic H2 production activity of H-CdS, H/C-CdS, C-CdS and H-CdS+C-CdS with 40 KHz frequency (LED lamp, 5 mW/cm2). (b) The piezo-photocatalytic H2 production rate of H-CdS, H/C-CdS, C-CdS and H-CdS+C-CdS with 40 KHz frequency (LED lamp, 5 mW/cm2). (c) The piezo-photocatalytic H2 production activity of H/C-CdS under natural sunlight with 40 KHz frequency (10 mW/cm2). (d) Fluorescence spectra of 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (TAOH) solution over H/C-CdS sample at different time. (e) The piezo-photocatalytic H2O2 generation for H/C-CdS. (f) Electron transfer numbers and H2O2 yields for H-CdS and H/C-CdS based on RRDE measurements.

Mechanistic analysis of piezo-photocatalytic process. The optimized structures of the hexagonal CdS (a) and phase junction CdS. (b) in the DFT calculations. (c) SHG generation for H-CdS and H/C-CdS. (d) Schematic diagram of mechanism for piezo-photocatalytic H2 production over H/C-CdS in pure water.

head.ce:title: Enhancing the macroscopic polarization of CdS for piezo-photocatalytic water splitting

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, Key Laboratory of Advanced Catalysis of Gansu Province, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Piezo-photocatalytic hydrogen production from water splitting is emerging as a promising way to generate renewable energy, but is challenged by low efficiency and ambiguous understanding of mechanisms. Herein, the CdS phase junction (H/C-CdS) with interfacial structural distortion is applied for prominent piezo-photocatalytic H2 evolution for the first time. Density functional theory (DFT) and second harmonic generation (SHG) demonstrate that the superior macroscopic polarity in H/C-CdS originates from the distortion of CdS4 tetrahedron units at the interface between hexagonal CdS (H-CdS) and cubic CdS (C-CdS). The obvious enhanced macroscopic polarity of H/C-CdS largely boosts the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes with the elimination of the electrostatic screening produced in CdS phase junction. An encouraging piezo-photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of 3.19 mmol·g−1·h−1 is delivered by H/C-CdS in pure water in the absence of any co-catalyst, which is 1.4 and 5.3 times higher than those of H-CdS (2.31 mmol·g−1·h−1) and C-CdS (0.59 mmol·g−1·h−1), respectively. Additionally, the value-added oxidation product H2O2 is produced in piezo-photocatalysis. This work firstly discloses macroscopic polarization enhancement induced by phase junction system on improvement of piezo-photocatalytic H2 evolution of CdS.

The construction of phase junction CdS is utilized for piezo-photocatalytic H2 evolution in pure water by collecting solar and mechanical energy. The tetrahedral distortion in phase junction CdS enhances the macroscopic polarization of hexagonal CdS and accelerates the separation of photoinduced charges, resulting in prominent piezo-photocatalytic H2 evolution performance.
ga1

ce:section.ce:para: Piezo-photocatalytic hydrogen production from water splitting has been a promising pathway to address the urgent demand for clean energy [1,2]. At present, optimization of hydrogen production activity by improving the polarization effect of piezoelectric semiconductor is becoming the center of attention [3]. However, realizing a dramatically enhanced H2 evolution performance using piezoelectric semiconductor by the structural engineering modulating macroscopic polarization has attracted much less attention.

Piezoelectrics is a class of non-centrosymmetric materials that produce a voltage when subjected to a mechanical stress [4]. The piezo-potential initiated by periodic mechanical energy or ultrasonic agitation provides a driving force for the transfer and separation of photoinduced charge carriers, thereby accelerating their separation. So far, various types of piezoelectric semiconductors such as ZnO, CdS, ZnSnO3 and BaTiO3 [5–9] have been extensively investigated for H2 evolution, CO2 reduction and degrading organic dyes by collecting solar and mechanical energy. Particularly, wurtzite CdS presents a noncentro-symmetric structure with a 6 mm point group [10]. The calculated bond length (2.605 Å) of the Cd-S bond along its [0001] direction in wurtzite CdS is slightly longer than the values in other directions (2.601 Å) through First-principle calculations [11], giving rise to the distortion of CdS4 tetrahedron in wurtzite CdS and the presence of electronic dipole moments. Hence, wurtzite CdS shows an inherent piezoelectric property [12–14] and supports the existence of piezoelectric polarization under mechanical strain along the [0001] direction. It was reported that wurtzite CdS nanorod arrays assembled on FTO substrates achieve a high H2 production rate of 20 µL∙h−1 under the optimized coupling field, which is much higher than that of unsupported samples [15].

Polarity is normally present in the non-centrosymmetric materials, such as piezoelectrics, pyroelectrics, ferroelectrics, and nonlinear optical materials [16]. For a non-centrosymmetric material, the polarization electric field provides a direct driving force to propel charge transfer and separation as a result of the shielding effect [17–19]. An ionic substitution strategy of replacing I5+ with V5+ increases the local dipole moments by promoting the structural distortion degree and enhances macroscopic polarization [20], which renders largely promoted photo- and piezoelectric induced reactive oxygen species (ROS) evolution. Recently, the anomalous piezoelectricity in g-C3N4 originated from the strong in-plane polarization along the a-axis affords an exceptional photo-piezocatalytic H2 evolution rate [2]. Considering the intrinsic polarity of wurtzite CdS, aggravating structure distortion may enhance macroscopic polarization of wurtzite CdS, and thus probably improve piezo-photocatalytic H2 evolution from pure water. Therefore, a flexible approach that regulates the structure distortion of wurtzite CdS is urgently needed.

Herein, the CdS phase junction (H/C-CdS) is prepared by a simple mixed solvothermal method and used for piezo-photocatalytic H2 evolution in pure water. The construction of CdS phase junction could increase structural asymmetry, considering the bond length differences of Cd-S bonds at the interface of H/C-CdS and induce promoted macroscopic polarization of wurtzite CdS, which directly contributes to the significantly enhanced separation of photoinduced carriers in piezo-photocatalysis. The H/C-CdS exhibits an unprecedented piezo-photocatalytic H2 evolution rate of 3.19 mmol·g−1·h−1 in pure water. Additionally, the value-added oxidation product H2O2 is generated over H/C-CdS along with H2 evolution.

Synthesis of CdS with different phases: Firstly, CdS phase junction is prepared according to the modest modification for the related literature [21]. In detail, 6.16 g CdSO4.8/3 H2O and 1.80 g thioacetamide were added into 80 mL of deionized water under stirring. Then, 3.2 mL ethanediamine was slowly added into the above solution and keep stirring for 30 min. The mixed solution was transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel reaction kettle and heated at 200 ℃ for 12 h. The resulting precipitate was collected by centrifugation and washed with ethanol and distilled water several times. Finally, CdS phase junction was obtained through vacuum drying. Moreover, other things being equal, hexagonal CdS was prepared by controlling Cd/S molar ratios of 4:1 and reaction time for 24 h. Cubic CdS was synthesized by regulating reaction temperature at 150 ℃ and reaction time for 8 h.

The X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8SSS) was conducted to determine the phase structures of the as-synthesized products. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were investigated via a VERTEX 70 spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany) using pressed KBr discs. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were performed by a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a Q600 SDT instrument (TA, USA) under N2 atmosphere from room temperature to 800 °C. The surface morphologies of the samples were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-4800) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai TF20). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were performed with an electron spectrometer (Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi). X-ray Absorption Near Edge Structure (XANES) data were collected on Beamline 4B7A at Beijing Synchrotron Radiation Facility (SSRF). Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded on FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments) with excitation at 340 nm. Second harmonics generation (SHG) tests were performed using a pulsed infrared beam (10 ns, 3 mJ, 10 kHz) produced by a Q-switched Nd:YAG laser at wavelength of 1064 nm.

At first, 2 mg catalyst was dispersed in 15 mL of an aqueous solution without sacrificial reagents. The reaction system was vacuumed using Ar for 15 min to remove any residual air in solution before piezo-photocatalytic reaction. Then, a continuous ultrasonic vibration from a 200 W ultrasonic generator (KQ5200B, 40 kHz) was applied at the bottom of the reactor during the reactor irradiated by a LED lamp. Eventually, the H2 content was analyzed by gas chromatography with a thermal conductivity detector equipped with 5 Å molecular sieve column.

The CdS phase junction was fabricated by directly treating CdSO4.8/3 H2O and thioacetamide precursor solution with properly controlled Cd/S molar ratios ( Fig. 1a). The synthesis of CdS phase junction is based on the fact that the growth mechanism of CdS largely depends on kinetics dominated or thermodynamics dominated process with the change of the Cd/S molar ratios [22]. As shown in Fig. 1b, three sharp X-ray diffraction (XRD) peaks at 24.81° (100), 26.25° (002) and 28.18° (101) are well-indexed to hexagonal CdS (JCPDS No.41–1049) [23]. XRD peaks of the synthesized CdS located at 26.5° (111), 43.9° (220) and 51.7° (311) support the existence of cubic CdS (JCPDS No.10–0454) [24]. The successful preparation of CdS phase junction is confirmed according to the characteristic diffraction peaks of hexagonal CdS and cubic CdS.

The FTIR spectra of the as-prepared CdS are displayed in Fig. S1. In the spectrum of H-CdS, two typical characteristic peaks at 1385 and 1113 cm−1 are assigned to the vibrations of Cd-S bonds [25], among which the peak position and intensity at 1385 cm−1 for H/C-CdS and C-CdS samples do not change compared with H-CdS. The peak (1097 cm−1) of C-CdS relative to H-CdS (1113 cm−1) is shifted to the lower wavenumber owing to the difference of the Cd-S bonds vibration direction [26]. In the spectrum of H/C-CdS, the peak (1113 cm−1) strength is greatly enhanced, which can be attributed to the increase of the dipole moment in H/C-CdS [27]. In addition, the UV–vis diffuse reflectance absorption spectra of the as-prepared CdS are presented in Fig. 1c. H/C-CdS has higher absorbance than C-CdS while the absorption edge appears an obvious red shift. Evidently, the construction of phase junction could affect the bandgap contact between C-CdS and H-CdS.

TG-DTA was carried out to verify the co-existence of hexagonal and cubic CdS (Fig. 1d). A slight weight loss (∼1.47%) occurs at around 250 °C due to the evaporation of absorbed water and ethanol [28]. The observed declined signal from the TG curve between 250 °C and 350 °C can be interpreted by the mass loss of elementary sulphur according to a previous report [28]. Afterwards, a relatively weak exothermic peak arises between 350 °C and 500 °C in the DTA curve without weight loss, which demonstrates the phase transition from hexagonal and cubic phases to a single stable hexagonal phase [24]. Furthermore, the phase co-existence is verified indirectly by the similar phase transition identified in XRD patterns (Fig. S2). Specifically, CdS phase junction that is obtained by heating for 12 h is transformed into a single hexagonal phase after another 12 h heating treatment.

The morphologies of the as-prepared CdS samples were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Fig. S3). Clearly, H-CdS sample mainly consists of irregular nanoparticles with the size ranges from 50 to 200 nm (Fig. S3a). The overall morphology (Fig. S3b) and the local 3D topography (Fig. 1e) of H/C-CdS reflect the irregular characteristics of phase junction CdS nanoparticles. By comparison, C-CdS shows smaller particle size (Fig. S3c).

The morphology and phase composition of H-CdS and H/C-CdS nanoparticles were examined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images. It can be found that H-CdS (Fig. S4a) and H/C-CdS (Fig. S5) present irregular particle morphology with uneven dimensions. The HRTEM images of H-CdS ( Figs. 2a and S4b) reveal fringes with lattice spacings of 0.32 nm, which can be indexed to the (101) lattice planes of hexagonal CdS. A highly uniform lattice distribution is discovered in the partial enlarged drawing of H-CdS sample (Fig. 2b). In Fig. 2c, the interplanar spacing of 0.25 nm is attributed to the (102) lattice plane of H-CdS and a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm is assigned to the (220) lattice plane of C-CdS. The lattice fringes with lattice spacing of 0.32 and 0.21 nm can be observed in the HRTEM image of H/C-CdS sample (Fig. 2d), which belong to the (101) lattice plane of H-CdS and (220) lattice plane of C-CdS, respectively. The apparent difference in lattice arrangements between H-CdS and C-CdS is clearly reflected by local magnification of H/C-CdS sample (Fig. 2e). In addition, the obvious lattice spacing of 0.25 and 0.29 nm (Fig. 2f) are also well matched with the (102) plane of H-CdS and (200) plane of C-CdS, respectively. Overall, the above characterization and analysis firmly prove the successful preparation of CdS phase junction.

Piezoresponse force microscopy (PFM) was carried out to illuminate the piezoelectric response of H/C-CdS. Dual alternating current resonance tracking (DART) modes were utilized to enhance the contrasts of the amplitude and phase images. Meanwhile, DART modes can avoid the electrostatic interaction contribution in displacement and the topographical interference in local electromechanical property mapping [29,30]. The topologies, amplitude and phase images of H/C-CdS nanoparticles are provided, respectively (Fig. S6). The H/C-CdS nanoparticle with irregular shapes is clearly observed in the topology (Fig. S6a). The amplitude image (Fig. S6b) exhibits clear contrast, reflecting different degrees of deformation. A clear contrast is detected from the phase image (Fig. S6c), which signifies different polarization directions and reveals a good piezoelectric response of H/C-CdS nanoparticle.

In the high resolution XPS spectra of Cd2+ for H-CdS ( Fig. 3a), two characteristic peaks located at 405.4 and 412.1 eV with a binding energy splitting of 6.7 eV indicate the presence of Cd2+ in H-CdS [31,32]. The two S 2p peaks at 161.7 and 162.8 eV correspond to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively, which can be attributed to typical S2- in H-CdS (Fig. 3b) [33]. Notable, the peak positions of Cd 3d and S 2p in C-CdS and H/C-CdS move slightly towards the lower binding energy as compared to those in H-CdS (Fig. 3a and b). The reason is that different crystal structures of CdS with phase discrepancy give rise to the difference in their chemical environment [34].

X-ray absorption near-edge structure (XANES) spectroscopy was conducted to probe the local structure of the S element in H-CdS, H/C-CdS and C-CdS. The peaks observed from Fig. 3c are due to the S L3,2 edges, S 2p3/2,1/2 to 4 s or 3d transitions [35]. Specifically, the S L-edge oscillation curves of H-CdS, H/C-CdS and C-CdS display obvious difference in the energy range of 160–190 eV. The peak at 185.8 eV for H/C-CdS is in between H-CdS (186.3 eV) and C-CdS (185.6 eV), indicating their different local atomic arrangements (Fig. 3c). It is in accordance with the fact that H-CdS and C-CdS have a slightly bond length difference [24].

In the S K-edge XANES spectra, the splits of peaks (2.47–2.48 KeV) are due to the distribution of the densities of state of the S p orbital [35]. The presence of negative bivalent S in H-CdS and H/C-CdS are confirmed by the white line energy at ~2.473 keV for sulfides (S-II) [36]. In addition, the H/C-CdS sample exhibits stronger the white line intensity than H-CdS, suggesting a higher S content in H/C-CdS compared to H-CdS.

Piezo-photocatalytic H2 evolution activity from water splitting over the as-prepared samples was evaluated by monitoring the amount of H2 evolution. Fig. 4a shows the time course of hydrogen over CdS with different phases under visible-light irradiation. The H2 production increases linearly with time for different samples. The H/C-CdS sample exhibits the highest H2 evolution rate with 3.19 mmol·g−1·h−1，which is 1.4 and 5.4 times higher than those of H-CdS (2.31 mmol·g−1·h−1) and C-CdS (0.59 mmol·g−1·h−1), respectively (Fig. 4b). Furthermore, the average H2 evolution rate for H/C-CdS achieves 2.5 times higher than that of the physical mixture of H-CdS and C-CdS (1.3 mmol·g−1·h−1). Evidently, the phase junction CdS shows stronger piezoelectric property than H-CdS and C-CdS, which significantly promotes the separation of photoinduced carriers. Note that H2 production performance over H/C-CdS in pure water under the coupling effect of ultrasonic vibration and light is significantly better than those of CdS based nanostructures under light illumination (Table S2).

The H2 evolution measurements were carried out under natural sunlight irradiation to estimate the practical application of H/C-CdS for water splitting (Fig. 4c). Under the ultrasonic vibration, the H/C-CdS gives a H2 evolution amount of 0.82 mmol·g−1 after 3 h sunlight irradiation. Nevertheless, no hydrogen is detected for H/C-CdS sample only under sunlight irradiation. Hence, a desired H2 evolution activity can be achieved under the couple effect of natural sunlight and ultrasonic vibration.

The effects of different conditions involving ultrasound, light and catalyst on H2 evolution were studied by performing comparative experiments (Fig. S7a, b). Without any catalyst, the ultrasound induces water to be cracked to produce trace hydrogen by the ultrasonic cavitation effect with and without light (Fig. S7a). No hydrogen is detected under visible light irradiation in the absence of H/C-CdS (Fig. S7a). A tiny amount of hydrogen is generated in the presence of H/C-CdS considering the intrinsic piezoelectric property of hexagonal CdS (Fig. S7b). The H/C-CdS sample is almost inactive for H2 generation only under irradiation (Fig. S7b). It is observed that piezo-photocatalytic H2 evolution rates over H/C-CdS present an increasing trend with increasing light intensity within a certain light intensity range (Fig. S8), indicating that H2 evolution activity is significantly dependent on light intensity in piezo-photocatalysis. The H2 production rate of H/C-CdS sample under coupling effect of ultrasound and light reaches 6 mmol·g−1·h−1 using methanol as the sacrifice reagent (Fig. S9), which is 55 times higher than that only under visible light (0.11 mmol·g−1·h−1). In brief, the synergistic effect of ultrasound and light contributes to the superior H2 production activity of H/C-CdS.

It is well-known that photocorrosion is an urgent problem to be solved when using sulfides as catalysts for H2 evolution in pure water. Herein, the cycling stabilities of the H-CdS and H/C-CdS samples were investigated to explore the inhibition effect of phase junction on photocorrosion (Fig. S11). As expected, the H2 evolution of H-CdS decreases by more than 34% after three cycles, reflecting the serious photocorrosion of H-CdS. In contrast, the H/C-CdS presents a moderate decrease (~14%) after three cycles. Besides, the crystallinity of the H/C-CdS nanoparticles is well maintained by XRD patterns (Fig. S12) before and after piezo-photocatalytic reaction. Hence, compared with the obvious declined H2 evolution activity in H-CdS, the construction of phase junction CdS can inhibit the photocorrosion of CdS, resulting in enhanced activity and stability of H/C-CdS from water splitting.

The oxidation products of water splitting by H/C-CdS were analyzed. The characteristic absorbance of 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid at 425 nm is detected on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Fig. 4d), which confirms the production of ·OH along with H2 evolution. This result reveals the mechanism of single electron oxidation of water to produce ·OH. Theoretically, the oxidation potential for OH- to ·OH is 2.1 V. However, the VB of CdS is situated at 1.8 V versus NHE at pH = 7 [37], indicating that the oxidation of water to ·OH by photogenerated holes of CdS is thermodynamically not favored. Whereas, it has been proposed that the active sites of a catalyst can induce the oxidation of water with lower energy demands by activating water molecules [38,39]. For H/C-CdS catalytic system, OH- in water molecules could be oxidized into ·OH through a single electron pathway from above discussion. Moreover, a small amount of H2O2 is generated under the piezo-photocatalytic reaction progress (Fig. 4e). Thus, the production of H2O2 might be ascribed to the combination of two ·OH. In short, the phase junction CdS simultaneously realizes the generation of H2 and H2O2 in pure water.

The rotating ring-disk electrode (RRDE) system was further utilized to elucidate the effect of the phase junction CdS on the production of H2O2 in photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting. The collection efficiency of the RRDE is measured to be 0.353 (Fig. S13 and Table S3). The electron-transfer number (n) and yield of H2O2 are then calculated according to the current-potential curves (Fig. S14) and the specific equations [40] given in the Supporting information. As a result, H-CdS gives an electron transfer number of 3.73–3.98 from 0.8 V to 1.8 V, while H/C-CdS has an electron transfer number of 3.68–3.98 in the same potential range (Fig. 4f). It has been proved that a four-electron transfer reaction tend to occur when the electron transfer number within the range of 3.85–3.96 [41,42]. Therefore, the reducing electron transfer number for H/C-CdS supports the production of H2O2 relative to the four-electron transfer mechanism. Additionally, the yield of H2O2 for H/C-CdS is higher than H-CdS at the range of 0.8–1.23 V vs NHE (Fig. 4f).

The impact of phase junction on the dipole moment of H-CdS was investigated by density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The optimized structure of the (101) planes of H-CdS was displayed in Fig. 5a. By contrast, the cubic CdS (220) surface with 4 atomic layers was placed on hexagonal phase CdS (101) surface to serve as a model for phase junction CdS, and the stable structure of H-CdS (101)/C-CdS (220) was depicted in Fig. 5b. The different atom configurations between H-CdS (101) plane and C-CdS (220) resulted in the symmetry broken of phase junction. The significant bond length fluctuations of Cd-S bonds within the interface between H-CdS and C-CdS induce the obvious distortion of CdS4 tetrahedron, which enhances the local structural disorder. As expected, Fig. 5a and b show the dependence of dipole moments on the symmetry of lattice structures, which are − 0.45 and 0.47 Debye along the (001) direction for the optimized H-CdS and H/C-CdS structures, respectively. A higher dipole moment would be effective to enhance the macroscopic polarization of CdS.

Subsequently, second harmonics generation (SHG) was performed (1064 nm Nd:YAG) to compare the macroscopic polarity of H-CdS and H/C-CdS. Obviously, the H/C-CdS presents a superior SHG response, beyond two times that of H-CdS (Fig. 5c), suggesting a significant enhanced macroscopic polarity in H/C-CdS. The macroscopic polarity enhancement in H/C-CdS would be beneficial for the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes.

The macroscopic polarization of H-CdS and H/C-CdS is further analyzed by polyhedral distortion index D. Polyhedral distortion can disturb the symmetry of coordination environment in crystal structure, leading to enhanced macroscopic polarization. The polyhedral distortion index D can be expressed by the following equation: [43].
D =
1 n
∑
i = 1
n
|
l
i
−
l
a v
|
l
a v
where l
i is the distance from the central atom to the ith ligands in a structural unit. l
av represents the average bond length of structural unit. The polyhedral distortion of CdS4 tetrahedron is determined to be 1.4 × 10−2 in H/C-CdS, which is bigger than that of H-CdS (2.1 × 10−4). Thus, the successful construction of phase junction induces prominent macroscopic polarization in H/C-CdS.

The electrochemical techniques were carried out to evaluate the transfer rate of photoinduced electron-hole pairs for the as prepared CdS samples. The photocurrent of H/C-CdS is much higher than that of H-CdS and C-CdS (Fig. S15), demonstrating the low recombination rate of photogenerated electrons and holes in H/C-CdS. It can be found that H/C-CdS has lower slope compared to H-CdS through the extrapolated straight lines of the Mott-Schottky plots at different frequencies (Fig. S16). Thus, the charge carrier concentration of H/C-CdS is higher than that of H-CdS, which supports the view that the formation of CdS phase junction effectively promotes charge transfer.

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of H-CdS and H/C-CdS appear two distinct emission bands at 545 nm and 630 nm (Fig. S17). The prominent emission band at 545 nm is related to the Stokes-shifted 1Σ exciton emission [44], and the relatively weak one at 630 nm stems from the recombination between conduction band (CB) electrons and trapped holes [45]. Compared with H-CdS, the intensity of the PL emission bands for H/C-CdS decreased evidently, which further confirms the low recombination rate of electrons and holes in H/C-CdS.

The mechanism of ultrasound-assisted charge separation in the photocatalytic process is revealed by comparing electrochemical performance of H/C-CdS with and without ultrasound. The photocurrent is enhanced when the ultrasound is applied on H/C-CdS and then returns to the original level once turning off the ultrasound (Fig. S18a). The above result consists well with the proposed assumption that photoinduced electrons and holes migrate along opposite directions under the ultrasound-induced piezoelectric field driven, ultimately leading to the accumulation of electrons on the catalyst’s surface. In addition, H/C-CdS exhibits lower slope under ultrasonic vibration compared with the plot without ultrasonic irradiation (Fig. S18b), indicating the higher concentration of charge carriers in H/C-CdS under ultrasonic vibration.

The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) Nyquist plots of H/C-CdS were carried out to assess the ultrasound-induced electric charge transfer (Fig. S19). The radii of the semicircles for H/C-CdS present a significant reduction with the increasement of ultrasound frequency, revealing the superior charge transfer efficiency under stronger ultrasonic vibration. The EIS results are fitted using an equivalent circuit (see inset in Fig. S19) and the fitted values are listed in Table S4. Specifically, the calculated charge-transfer resistance (Rct) of H/C-CdS with a high ultrasound frequency (40 KHz) is apparently lower than that of H/C-CdS with a low ultrasound frequency (20 KHz), which confirms that the built-in electric field induced by ultrasound significantly promotes the separation of photoinduced electric charges and results in a reduced resistance value.

The band gap (Eg) values of the H-CdS and C-CdS are determined as 2.37 and 2.32 eV, respectively (Fig. S20a). According to the Mott-Schottky plots of H-CdS and C-CdS presented in Fig. S20b, the resulted flat-band potential values are around − 0.74 and − 0.67 V vs. Ag/AgCl, respectively, for H-CdS and C-CdS, i.e. − 0.54 and − 0.47 V vs. NHE, respectively. Hence, the conduction band (CB) of H-CdS and C-CdS are about − 0.64 and − 0.57 V vs. NHE, respectively. Correspondingly, the valence band (VB) of H- CdS and C-CdS are calculated to be 1.73 and 1.75 V vs. NHE, respectively. Consequently, energy-level alignment shows that the band edge positions of H-CdS and C-CdS match well with the conditions of n-n type heterostructures.

On the basis of the above results, the possible piezo-photocatalytic H2 evolution mechanism is speculated in Fig. 5d. When the H/C-CdS is illuminated under visible light, photoinduced electrons migrate from VB of H-CdS and C-CdS to their CB, respectively. Then, photoinduced electrons in the CB of H-CdS transfer to the CB of C-CdS by the internal electric field resulted from the phase junction, while photoinduced holes in the VB of C-CdS move to the VB of H-CdS. Despite the built-in electric field of CdS phase junction can effectively facilitate the separation of photoinduced electrons and holes, H/C-CdS still presents a poor H2 production activity without any sacrificial reagents. This can be explained from two aspects: (1) A static built-in electric field can be easily saturated by free carriers due to electrostatic screening [46], thus restricting the continuous separation of photoinduced carriers. (2) The impact of CdS phase junction on accelerating photogenerated carriers separation is only confined to the vicinity of the interface between H-CdS and C-CdS, resulting in low carrier utilization efficiency.

After an elevated pressure field supplied by ultrasound conducted on the H/C-CdS under visible light, the piezoelectric polarization of the H/C-CdS leads to the generation of the bound surface charges and induces a built-in electric field inside the H/C-CdS. The built-in electric fields propel the photogenerated electrons and holes to migrate toward opposite directions, thus resulting in more electrons to reduce H+ in H2O to produce H2. Meanwhile, photogenerated holes oxidize OH- in water molecules to produce ·OH through a single electron pathway and then the combination of two ·OH generates H2O2.

On account of the much stronger macroscopic polarization of H/C-CdS than H-CdS, higher charge separation occur in H/C-CdS sample. Additionally, the electrostatic screening produced in CdS phase junction can be well eliminated by the existence of the piezoelectric polarization field, thereby ensuring continuous separation of photogenerated carriers. In brief, the synergistic effect between the formation of phase junction and enhanced macroscopic polarization in catalyst H/C-CdS greatly accelerates the separation of photoinduced carriers and prominently inhibits photocorrosion of CdS, affording remarkable H2 production in pure water.

In this work, CdS phase junction with interfacial structural distortion achieves an exceptional piezo-photocatalytic H2 evolution in pure water. Meanwhile, the value-added oxidation product H2O2 is generated while H2 evolution in piezo-photocatalysis. DFT calculation suggests that the CdS4 tetrahedral distortion at the interface between H-CdS and C-CdS results in the increased dipole moment of H/C-CdS, signifying the stronger macroscopic polarization of H/C-CdS than H-CdS. Besides, the enhancement of the macroscopic polarization for H/C-CdS is corroborated by second harmonics generation and polyhedral distortion index D. Overall, the enhanced macroscopic polarization in H/C-CdS induces higher charge separation efficiency, contributing to an unprecedented H2 evolution in pure water. This study indicates the promising prospect of macroscopic polarization enhancement originated from phase junction system on promoting piezo-photocatalytic H2 evolution.

Meiyu Zhang: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. Siyang Nie: Formal analysis, Software. Tao Cheng: Investigation, Methodology. Yu Feng: Investigation, Methodology. Chenchen Zhang: Formal analysis, Validation. Lei Zheng: Methodology. Liang Wu: Software. Weichang Hao: Formal analysis. Yong Ding: Project administration, Resources, Methodology, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China, China (Grant no. 21773096, 22075119) and the Natural Science Foundation of Gansu Province, China (21JR7RA440).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nanoen.2021.106635.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Meiyu Zhang received her M.Sc. degree from College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Huaibei Normal University in 2017. She is currently studying for a doctoral degree in Prof. Yong Ding’s Group. Her current interests include the synthesis and characterization of nano-catalysts for piezo-photocatalytic and pyroelectric-catalytic hydrogen evolution.

Siyang Nie received his Ph. D in School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering from Shanghai Jiao Tong University in 2021. Then, he joined Shanghai Jiao Tong University as postdoctoral fellow in 2021. Currently, Dr Nie has broad interests in nanoporous materials, surface science and first-principle calculations of heterogeneous catalysis.

Tao Cheng received his B.S. degree from College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Lanzhou University in 2020. Now, he is a M.A. student at Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. He is currently working on the in-depth research and advancement on structure, electrolyte and Zn anode of Zn-based batteries including Zn-bromine batteries (ZBBs) and Zn-ion batteries (ZIBs).

Yu Feng received her B.S. degree from College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Central China Normal University in 2021. Now, she is a M.A. student at Lanzhou University under the supervision of Prof. Yong Ding. She is currently working on the synthesis and characterization of polyoxometalates (POMs).

Chenchen Zhang received her B.S. degree from College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering at Lanzhou University in 2019. Now, she is a M.A. student at Lanzhou University under the supervision of Prof. Yong Ding. Her main research interests focus on the synthesis and characterization of nano-catalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER).

Lei Zheng is an associate researcher at the Institute of High Energy Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences (IHEP, CAS). He received a bachelor's degree in theoretical physics from the Physics Department of Shandong University in 2000. In 2005, he obtained a Ph.D. in optics from IHEP, CAS. His main work focuses on the tender X-ray absorption spectroscopy methodology and X-ray metrology. He is responsible for BSRF's 4B7A tender X-ray beamline, which has attracted a large number of synchrotron users to carry out absorption spectrum measurement research in the energy, materials and environmental fields in this energy region.

Liang Wu received his Ph.D. in chemical engineering from Imperial College London in 2013 under the supervision of Prof. George Jackson and Prof. Erich Müller. He joined Shanghai Jiao Tong University as postdoctoral fellow late 2014 and then assistant researcher in the theoretical and computational chemistry group of Prof. Huai Sun where he conducts multiscale modelling and simulation of soft matter, catalyst and polymers.

Weichang Hao received his Ph.D. degree from Lanzhou University in 2003. He is currently a distinguished professor at the School of Physics, Beihang University. His main research interests focus on electronic structures of oxide materials, surface physics and chemistry and novel two-dimensional quantum materials.

Yong Ding received his Ph.D. degree in Physical Chemistry from Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, in 2005. Then, he joined the College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Lanzhou University. In 2009, he went to Emory University as a visiting scholar. When he returned back Lanzhou University in 2011, he was promoted to a full professor. He is now the director of the institute of physical chemistry of Lanzhou University and is Feitian scholar distinguished professor of Gansu Province. He joined the editorial board of Chinese Journal of Catalysis in 2020 and Chinese Chemical Letters in 2021. He has published about 130 peer-reviewed papers in primary SCI journals and gave about 50 invited lectures at various scientific conferences so far. Ding’s main research interests are as follows: Artificial photosynthesis; 2) Water splitting by polyoxometalates, metal complexes and metal oxides; 3) Photocatalytic conversion of carbon dioxide; 4) Synthesis, characterization and catalytic applications of polyoxometalates.
